title: Multiple failed login attempts detected
description: Splunk SIEM fired a correlation rule after detecting 847 failed authentication events against auth-service within a 12-minute window, all targeting distinct user accounts from a single IP range (45.142.212.0/24 — known Tor exit nodes). The pattern matched a credential-stuffing signature: sequential account enumeration with a 1.4-second inter-request delay consistent with automated tooling. No successful authentications from the attacking IP range were detected. The attack targeted the /api/v2/auth/login endpoint without triggering rate limiting, as the gateway's per-IP rate limit (200 req/min) was being evaded by distributing requests across 6 source IPs within the same subnet.
detection_method: Splunk SIEM — correlation rule 'AUTH-002: Distributed credential stuffing pattern' (>500 failed logins across >100 accounts in 10 min from <10 source IPs)

timeline.event: Splunk SIEM rule AUTH-002 fired: 847 failed logins targeting 312 unique accounts from 6 Tor exit node IPs (45.142.212.x) in 12 minutes against /api/v2/auth/login; no successful logins detected
On-call security analyst k.tremblay@company.com acknowledged alert; confirmed attacking IP range 45.142.212.0/24 listed in Tor Project exit node list; immediate block applied at AWS WAF level

Root cause confirmed: auth-service rate limiting was per-IP (200 req/min) with no global endpoint rate limit; attacker distributed requests across 6 IPs to stay below per-IP threshold; 0 successful authentications confirmed in CloudWatch logs

Auth-service rate limiting updated: global endpoint rate limit of 500 req/min added; CAPTCHA challenge injected after 5 consecutive failed logins per account regardless of source IP; 312 targeted accounts sent password reset advisories
Tor exit node blocklist added to AWS WAF managed rule set; attack traffic fully mitigated; monitoring confirmed no further credential stuffing attempts; incident closed

resolution_summary: AWS WAF blocked the 45.142.212.0/24 IP range within 2 minutes of detection, stopping the active attack. Authentication logs confirmed 0 successful logins from the attacking infrastructure across all 847 attempts. The root rate limiting gap (per-IP only, no global endpoint limit) was patched: a global 500 req/min limit and per-account CAPTCHA challenge after 5 failures were implemented. All 312 targeted accounts received a proactive password reset advisory. The Tor exit node blocklist was added as a permanent AWS WAF managed rule.
root_cause_analysis: The auth-service API gateway enforced rate limiting at 200 requests/minute per source IP — an effective control against single-source attacks but trivially bypassed by distributed credential stuffing. The attacker used 6 Tor exit nodes from the 45.142.212.0/24 range, each sending 140-160 requests per minute, keeping each IP below the per-IP threshold while collectively generating 847 requests. No global endpoint rate limit existed on /api/v2/auth/login. The credential list used appears to be a publicly available combo list — no company-specific credentials were in the list, as only 0.4% of the 312 targeted accounts had ever used passwords from the list-derived patterns based on Splunk analysis of the endpoint error codes (all returned 401, none returned 403, indicating no lockouts were triggered either).
business_impact: Moderate security risk, limited exposure, potential for escalation if not addressed
remediation_actions: Immediately blocked IP range 45.142.212.0/24 via AWS WAF custom rule: aws wafv2 update-ip-set --id <tor-blocklist-id> — attack stopped within 90 seconds of block application
Confirmed 0 successful authentications: SELECT * FROM cloudwatch_auth_logs WHERE source_ip IN ('45.142.212.*') AND result = 'SUCCESS' — returned 0 rows across all 847 attempts
Applied AWS WAF managed rule 'AWSManagedRulesBotControlRuleSet' to /api/v2/auth/login endpoint to enable automatic bot traffic detection and blocking
Updated auth-service API gateway config: added global endpoint rate limit of 500 req/min on /api/v2/auth/login regardless of per-IP distribution; deployed in 2 hours
Implemented per-account progressive CAPTCHA: 5 consecutive failed logins (any source IPs) triggers Google reCAPTCHA v3 challenge on next attempt
Sent proactive advisory to all 312 targeted accounts recommending password change; included HaveIBeenPwned check link; 89% completed password reset within 24 hours
Added 45.142.212.0/24 and full Tor exit node list (updated daily via Tor Project feed) to permanent AWS WAF IP blocklist
preventive_measures: Global endpoint rate limiting deployed on all /auth/ endpoints: 500 req/min aggregate cap with 429 response and Retry-After header
AWS WAF Bot Control managed rule set enabled for all authentication endpoints — requests from known bots and automation frameworks now blocked pre-authentication
Okta customer accounts migrated: all users now required to enroll in Okta MFA (TOTP minimum) — credential stuffing against MFA-enrolled accounts cannot succeed without OTP
Splunk rule AUTH-002 sensitivity tuned: now fires at 200 failed logins from <10 IPs in 5 minutes (from previous 500 in 10 min), enabling 2x faster detection
Breach password check integrated into auth-service: passwords matching HaveIBeenPwned database (k-anonymity API) are rejected at login with forced reset prompt
security_controls_updated: AWS WAF updated: Tor exit node list managed rule enabled for all public API endpoints; updated daily from Tor Project exit node feed
Auth-service rate limiting redesigned: per-IP limit (200/min) supplemented with global endpoint limit (500/min) and per-account ban-on-5-failures policy
Okta MFA enforcement policy updated: mandatory MFA for all new and existing user accounts; grace period for enrollment ends 2025-10-23
SIEM correlation rule AUTH-002 sensitivity doubled: alert threshold lowered from 500 to 200 failed logins across 50+ accounts in 5 minutes
documentation_updated: Authentication Security Runbook updated with credential stuffing detection procedure, AWS WAF IP block command, and account notification template
Rate Limiting Architecture Document updated with global endpoint limiting design and bot control managed rule configuration
Credential Stuffing Incident Response Playbook created as standalone document covering detection, blocking, account notification, and post-incident analysis steps
Incident logged as SEC-4311 Jira ticket; attack IP range and credential list patterns archived in threat intelligence database
lessons_learned: Per-IP rate limiting alone is not a sufficient defense against distributed credential stuffing — any attacker with access to a few IPs (trivially available via Tor, residential proxies, or cloud VMs) can stay below per-IP thresholds indefinitely. Global endpoint rate limiting and per-account failed-login tracking are the correct primitives. The most durable control is MFA enforcement: even if the attacker had succeeded in finding a valid username/password pair, MFA would have prevented any actual account takeover. The Splunk rule's original threshold of 500 failed logins was too high — the attack would have been at 847 attempts before detection; a 200-login threshold with 5-minute window would have caught it at the 200th attempt.
follow_up_tasks: AUTH-1009-01: Complete mandatory MFA enrollment for all 6,800 customer accounts — enforce hard cutoff 2025-10-23 — Owner: Product Security, Due: 2025-10-23
AUTH-1009-02: Integrate HaveIBeenPwned k-anonymity API for breach password checking at login and registration — Owner: Platform, Due: 2025-10-30
AUTH-1009-03: Implement Tor exit node feed auto-update in AWS WAF (daily refresh via Lambda) — Owner: Cloud Security, Due: 2025-10-16
AUTH-1009-04: Tune Splunk AUTH-002 rule and backtest against 6-month historical data to validate sensitivity — Owner: Detection Engineering, Due: 2025-10-20
resolution: Zero accounts compromised; attack blocked at AWS WAF; 312 targeted accounts sent password reset advisories; global rate limiting and bot control deployed
action_taken: Resolved by security-team within 309 minutes of Splunk SIEM detection
detection_gap: No per-IP rate limiting existed on the authentication endpoint; Tor exit node IP addresses were not pre-blocked at the WAF layer; no velocity alerting was configured for failed authentication attempts below individual account lockout thresholds — approximately 850 attempts from a single IP range were needed before the SIEM rule threshold was breached
what_went_well: Okta ThreatInsight automatically began blocking authentication requests from Tor exit nodes within minutes of policy activation
Cross-reference with the Breach Compilation credential database identified all 23 at-risk accounts within 45 minutes, enabling targeted MFA enforcement
MFA enforcement on all 23 targeted accounts prevented any successful authentication despite the attacker possessing valid password matches for 4 accounts
what_went_wrong: Authentication endpoint lacked per-IP rate limiting — approximately 850 attempts from a single IP range proceeded unchallenged until the SIEM velocity threshold was crossed
Tor exit node IPs were not pre-blocked at the WAF layer despite Tor exit node traffic being a known high-risk signal for credential stuffing
Account lockout policy was configured per-account rather than per-IP, allowing the attacker to distribute attempts across accounts without triggering individual lockouts
